text: The gorilla experiment is often cited as a quirky curiosity, but its implications extend into life-and-death domains. Studies of aviation accidents have found that pilots, heads-down on instrument scans, have failed to notice terrain-clearance warnings. Radiologists scanning for tumors have missed inserted images of a gorilla-sized figure in lung scans — a finding from Trafton Drew and colleagues in 2013. Expert training sharpens attention in specific directions but simultaneously creates predictable blind spots. The more skilled a professional becomes at detecting what they expect, the more vulnerable they may be to what falls outside their trained expectation. Expertise, paradoxically, can narrow the attentional aperture.
question: What did Trafton Drew's 2013 study find about radiologists?

options.text: Expert radiologists outperformed novices in detecting unexpected objects in scans
Radiologists scanning for tumors missed a gorilla-sized figure inserted into lung scans
Radiologists were more susceptible to change blindness than to inattentional blindness
Expertise in radiology eliminated attentional blind spots over time
